Threat.value: Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience
Being superalert or watchful or on guard
Having strong negative feelings such as fear, horror, anger, guilt, or shame

Negative Appraisals.value: Somebody else would not have gotten into this situation
I have to be on guard all the time
People are not what they seem

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I dwell on how the event could have been prevented

Memory.value: My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.
My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.
My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.

vignette: As a non-binary individual from Oceania, aged 44, this patient's life has been marked by a profound sense of loss and trauma. The suicide of a loved one has left an indelible mark on their psyche, manifesting in a complex array of symptoms that permeate every aspect of their daily existence. Gunshot noises, for instance, can instantly transport them back to the fateful day, triggering a cascade of physical reactions - racing heart, sweaty palms, and an overwhelming sense of dread. These triggers serve as a constant reminder of the trauma, fuelling a pervasive sense of threat that dominates their waking hours. They are perpetually on edge, hypervigilant and watchful, as if waiting for the next catastrophe to strike. This state of heightened arousal is accompanied by intense negative emotions - fear, anger, guilt, and shame - which can be overwhelming at times. The patient's self-comparison tendencies also come into play, with them often wondering if someone else would have been better equipped to handle the situation. As a result of these distorted appraisals, the patient has developed a range of coping strategies that, while providing temporary relief, ultimately exacerbate their condition. They have become increasingly avoidant, steering clear of hospitals, ambulances, and physical examinations - anything that might remind them of the traumatic event. Similarly, they have begun to shy away from intimate relationships, finding it difficult to engage in sex or form meaningful connections with others. Ruminations about prevention also occupy a significant amount of their mental space, with the patient frequently replaying the events leading up to the suicide in an attempt to identify what could have been done differently. These maladaptive strategies have taken a toll on the patient's relationships and overall quality of life. Their divorce, for instance, can be seen as a consequence of their increasing withdrawal from social interactions. The patient's visual memories of the traumatic event remain vivid and detailed, often replaying like a film in their mind. This has led to a deep-seated distrust of others, with the patient frequently wondering if people are genuinely what they seem. Despite these challenges, the patient remains committed to seeking help and working through their trauma. Their experiences serve as a poignant reminder of the devastating impact that traumatic events can have on an individual's life. Through therapy, it is hoped that the patient will be able to reframe their distorted appraisals, develop more adaptive coping strategies, and eventually learn to navigate their memories without becoming overwhelmed by the associated emotions.
